pattern: Because removal only ever happens in same-value groups of two or three, each distinct value's count can be solved independently with a small greedy rule, and the answers simply add up.
hints: Group the array by value and look at each value's count in isolation — how you clear one value's copies never interacts with any other value's copies.
A count of exactly 1 can never be cleared (you cannot form a group of two or three from a single leftover copy), so spot that case immediately and report the whole answer as impossible.

For any count of 2 or more, using as many groups of three as possible and mopping up the remainder with groups of two is always optimal, which is equivalent to taking the ceiling of the count divided by three; sum that over every distinct value.

edge_inputs.description: a single leftover copy makes the whole array impossible to clear
a count of four needs two operations, not three
mixed counts where one value's count of exactly one poisons the whole answer
minimum length array, a lone unpairable value
minimum length array, an immediately pairable value
several distinct values with counts that are exact multiples of three, tempting a naive count-divided-by-two shortcut
a count of seven checks the ceiling-of-thirds rounding at an odd remainder

stress.description: random values with a small alphabet so counts per value get large, some values may land on an unpairable count of one
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 5000))
    span = max(1, n // 3)
    nums = [rng.randint(1, span) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]

stress.description: random values constructed so every distinct value's count is at least two, guaranteeing a solvable instance
